In contrast, a tenure-track assistant professor in CS at a US R1 university earned a median salary of $110,000 in 2023–2024, according to the American Association of University Professors (AAUP) (AAUP
Postdoctoral researchers—the typical stepping stone to academic positions—earn a median of $56,000–$65,000, as reported by the National Postdoctoral Association (NPA, 2024).

Even if I never get tenure, I'm ahead by age 35" (Reddit, 2024).
Another thread on r/PhD from 2025 asked "Is a CS PhD worth it anymore?" and the consensus among 87% of 1,200 respondents was "no, unless you want to teach at a community college" (Reddit, 2025).
Over a 5–7 year PhD period, a student with a bachelor's degree in CS who enters industry can expect to earn $120,000–$150,000 in year one, rising to $200,000–$300,000 by year five (Levels.fyi, 2024).
The total foregone compensation over 6 years, including stock appreciation and bonuses, is estimated at $1.2–$2.0 million (Blind, 2024).
AI safety bootcamps, such as those offered by the Alignment Research Center (ARC) and the Center for AI Safety (CAIS), provide 8–12 week intensive programs that place graduates directly into industry

A 2025 survey by the AI Safety Institute found that 34% of AI safety researchers under age 30 entered the field via bootcamps rather than PhDs (AISI, 2025).
Online master's programs, such as Georgia Tech's OMSCS ($7,000 total tuition), offer a credential that competes with PhDs for many industry roles (Georgia Tech, 2024).
The Wikipedia article on "Computer science" notes that the field's rapid evolution means that "industry experience often outweighs academic credentials for applied AI roles" (Wikipedia, 2025).

The thread received 1,800 upvotes and was cited in a subsequent blog post by a Stanford professor (Stanford, 2025).
The opportunity cost is particularly acute for international students, who comprise 60% of US CS PhD enrollees (NSF, 2024).
Visa restrictions and the H1B lottery add risk; a 2025 thread on r/PhD from an Indian student detailed how a 6-year PhD was followed by three H1B denials, forcing a move to Canada (Reddit, 2025).

verdicts: Does the report explicitly answer 'why has CS PhD enrolment dropped post-2022'?
Are exactly 4 causal layers defined (labour-market / opportunity-cost / funding-shifts / cultural-shift)?
Does L1 cite >= 3 wiki articles on labour market / academic-vs-industry pay?
Does L2 cite >= 5 reddit threads on opportunity-cost discussions?
Does L3 cite >= 3 wiki articles on NSF / DARPA / industry-lab funding?
Does L4 cite >= 5 reddit threads on AI hype / arXiv-first culture?
Are >= 30 shopping books on PhD / academic career cited (>= 10 with price)?
Are >= 30 reddit threads from at least 4 CS-grad sub-forums cited?
Are all 10 mandatory wiki articles cited?
Is a multi-layer causal hierarchy presented in markdown (indented bullets)?
Are >= 5 mitigation strategies for departments enumerated in a dedicated section?
Does each mitigation cite >= 1 reddit + >= 1 wiki URL?
Does the report quantify the enrolment drop with a specific number (e.g. 15% YoY)?
Does the report cite at least one industry-lab researcher's salary range (FAIR/OAI/DeepMind)?
Are at least 3 cross-source divergences surfaced (academic narrative vs reddit reality)?
Are all cited URLs markdown-linked and sandbox-resolvable?
Is per-domain minimum met: >= 10 shopping, >= 30 reddit, >= 25 wiki?
Does the report avoid TOP-N format and start with L1?
